is an American professional [soccer](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") club based in the [Dallas–Fort Worth metroplex](/wiki/Dallas%E2%80%93Fort_Worth_metroplex "Dallas–Fort Worth metroplex"). The club competes in [Major League Soccer](/wiki/Major_League_Soccer "Major League Soccer") (MLS) as a member of the [Western Conference](/wiki/Western_Conference_(MLS) "Western Conference (MLS)"). The franchise begun playing in 1996 as a charter club of the league. The club was founded in 1995 as the **Dallas Burn** before adopting its current name in 2004.

**FC Dallas** is an American professional [soccer](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") club based in the [Dallas–Fort Worth metroplex](/wiki/Dallas%E2%80%93Fort_Worth_metroplex "Dallas–Fort Worth metroplex"). The club competes in [Major League Soccer](/wiki/Major_League_Soccer "Major League Soccer") (MLS) as a member of the [Western Conference](/wiki/Western_Conference_(MLS) "Western Conference (MLS)"). The franchise begun playing in 1996 as a charter club of the league. The club was founded in 1995 as the **Dallas Burn** before adopting its current name in 2004.

Since 2005, Dallas have played in the DFW area's northern suburbs at the 20,500-capacity [soccer-specific](/wiki/Soccer-specific_stadium "Soccer-specific stadium") [Toyota Stadium](/wiki/Toyota_Stadium_(Texas) "Toyota Stadium (Texas)") in [Frisco, Texas](/wiki/Frisco,_Texas "Frisco, Texas"); home games in the club's early years were played at the [Cotton Bowl](/wiki/Cotton_Bowl_(stadium) "Cotton Bowl (stadium)"). The team is owned by the Hunt Sports Group led by brothers [Clark Hunt](/wiki/Clark_Hunt "Clark Hunt") and Dan Hunt, who is the team's president. The [Hunt family](/wiki/Hunt_family_(Texas) "Hunt family (Texas)") also owns the [NFL](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League")'s [Kansas City Chiefs](/wiki/Kansas_City_Chiefs "Kansas City Chiefs") and part of the [Chicago Bulls](/wiki/Chicago_Bulls "Chicago Bulls").

FC Dallas in [2016](/wiki/2016_Major_League_Soccer_season "2016 Major League Soccer season") won their first [Supporters' Shield](/wiki/Supporters%27_Shield "Supporters' Shield"). In [2010](/wiki/2010_MLS_Cup "2010 MLS Cup") they were runners-up in the MLS Cup, losing to the [Colorado Rapids](/wiki/Colorado_Rapids "Colorado Rapids") in extra time. The team has won the [U.S. Open Cup](/wiki/Lamar_Hunt_U.S._Open_Cup "Lamar Hunt U.S. Open Cup") on two occasions (in [1997](/wiki/1997_U.S._Open_Cup "1997 U.S. Open Cup") and again in [2016](/wiki/2016_Lamar_Hunt_U.S._Open_Cup "2016 Lamar Hunt U.S. Open Cup")). Their fully owned USL affiliate, [North Texas SC](/wiki/North_Texas_SC "North Texas SC"), won the 2019 [USL League One](/wiki/USL_League_One "USL League One") regular season and overall championship titles, the third division title in American soccer. The [International Federation of Football History & Statistics](/wiki/International_Federation_of_Football_History_%26_Statistics "International Federation of Football History & Statistics"), in its Club World Ranking for the year ending December 31, 2016, placed FC Dallas as the 190th best club in the world and the ninth best club in CONCACAF.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

The Toros' academy is reputed for its player development, having produced several players who have gone on to feature for European clubs and the [United States men's national soccer team](/wiki/United_States_men%27s_national_soccer_team "United States men's national soccer team") such as [Weston McKennie](/wiki/Weston_McKennie "Weston McKennie"), [Reggie Cannon](/wiki/Reggie_Cannon "Reggie Cannon"), [Ricardo Pepi](/wiki/Ricardo_Pepi "Ricardo Pepi"), and [Chris Richards](/wiki/Chris_Richards_(soccer) "Chris Richards (soccer)").[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Dallas&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Dallas Burn era: 1996–2004

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Dallas&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Dallas Burn era: 1996–2004")]

Dallas was awarded a Major League Soccer franchise on June 6, 1995, the same day as teams were awarded to [Kansas City](/wiki/Sporting_Kansas_City "Sporting Kansas City") and Colorado.[[6]](#cite_note-mls96-6) The team was given its name for the burning in the Texan oilfields and the state's hot weather.[[7]](#cite_note-fifa-7) On October 17, former [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico_national_football_team "Mexico national football team") international [Hugo Sánchez](/wiki/Hugo_S%C3%A1nchez "Hugo Sánchez") was designated to the team as their first player.[[6]](#cite_note-mls96-6) Initially not attracting investors,[[7]](#cite_note-fifa-7) the Burn was financed by the league itself.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/Chicago_Fire_versus_Dallas_Burn_%28JUL-1998%29.jpg/250px-Chicago_Fire_versus_Dallas_Burn_%28JUL-1998%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chicago_Fire_versus_Dallas_Burn_(JUL-1998).jpg)

Dallas Burn (in white) playing against Chicago Fire at [Soldier Field](/wiki/Soldier_Field "Soldier Field") in July 1998

On April 14, 1996, the Dallas Burn played their first game, defeating the [San Jose Clash](/wiki/San_Jos%C3%A9_Earthquakes "San José Earthquakes") in a shootout win in front of a crowd of 27,779 fans at the [Cotton Bowl](/wiki/Cotton_Bowl_(stadium) "Cotton Bowl (stadium)").[[6]](#cite_note-mls96-6) Five days later, [Jason Kreis](/wiki/Jason_Kreis "Jason Kreis") scored the team's first goal in a 3–0 home win over the [Wiz](/wiki/Sporting_KC "Sporting KC").[[9]](#cite_note-9) With a record of 17–15, the Burn finished in second in the [Western Conference](/wiki/Western_Conference_(MLS) "Western Conference (MLS)") behind the [Los Angeles Galaxy](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Galaxy "Los Angeles Galaxy"). They lost in the best of three playoff semi-finals to the Wiz after three games, the last one being decided by a shootout.[[6]](#cite_note-mls96-6) Their first campaign in the [U.S. Open Cup](/wiki/U.S._Open_Cup "U.S. Open Cup") ended with a 2–3 home defeat in the semi-finals against [D.C. United](/wiki/D.C._United "D.C. United").[[10]](#cite_note-10) In their second season, the Burn again reached the [playoffs](/wiki/1997_MLS_Cup_Playoffs "1997 MLS Cup Playoffs"), where they lost in the conference finals to the [Colorado Rapids](/wiki/Colorado_Rapids "Colorado Rapids").[[11]](#cite_note-11) Later in [1997](/wiki/1997_U.S._Open_Cup "1997 U.S. Open Cup"), they won their first U.S. Open Cup by defeating the MLS Cup champions, D.C. United.[[12]](#cite_note-12) In 1999, [striker](/wiki/Striker_(football) "Striker (football)") Kreis was voted the league's [MVP](/wiki/Major_League_Soccer_MVP "Major League Soccer MVP") for a season in which he became the first player to reach 15 goals and 15 assists.[[13]](#cite_note-13) That season ended in the playoffs with a defeat to the Galaxy in the conference finals.[[14]](#cite_note-14) In October 2000, head coach [Dave Dir](/wiki/Dave_Dir "Dave Dir") was fired, despite again taking the team to the [playoffs](/wiki/2000_MLS_Cup_Playoffs "2000 MLS Cup Playoffs") for the fifth consecutive time.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

Dir's replacement in January 2001 was [Mike Jeffries](/wiki/Mike_Jeffries_(soccer) "Mike Jeffries (soccer)"), who had won the [1998 MLS Cup](/wiki/1998_MLS_Cup "1998 MLS Cup") and two U.S. Open Cups with the [Chicago Fire](/wiki/Chicago_Fire_Soccer_Club "Chicago Fire Soccer Club").[[16]](#cite_note-16) In his first season in charge, which was cut short as a result of the [September 11 attacks](/wiki/September_11_attacks "September 11 attacks"), Dallas lost in the [playoff](/wiki/2001_MLS_Cup_Playoffs "2001 MLS Cup Playoffs") quarterfinals to Jeffries' former team.[[17]](#cite_note-17) They were also eliminated in the second round of the [2001 U.S. Open Cup](/wiki/2001_U.S._Open_Cup "2001 U.S. Open Cup") by the [Seattle Sounders Select](/wiki/Seattle_Sounders_Select "Seattle Sounders Select"), an amateur team from the third-tier [Premier Development League](/wiki/Premier_Development_League "Premier Development League").[[18]](#cite_note-18) The 2002 season ended with a third-place finish in the West and overall for Dallas, along with an early playoffs exit to the [Colorado Rapids](/wiki/Colorado_Rapids "Colorado Rapids").[[19]](#cite_note-19)
For the [2003 season](/wiki/2003_Major_League_Soccer_season "2003 Major League Soccer season"), the Burn relocated their home games from the Cotton Bowl to the much lower capacity [Dragon Stadium](/wiki/Dragon_Stadium,_Southlake "Dragon Stadium, Southlake") (a high school football stadium) in [Southlake](/wiki/Southlake,_Texas "Southlake, Texas"), which is a northern [Fort Worth](/wiki/Fort_Worth,_Texas "Fort Worth, Texas") suburb.[[20]](#cite_note-our-20) The team performed poorly in 2003 and Jeffries was fired in September. He was temporarily replaced by his assistant, former [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_national_football_team "Northern Ireland national football team") international [Colin Clarke](/wiki/Colin_Clarke_(footballer_born_1962) "Colin Clarke (footballer born 1962)").[[21]](#cite_note-21) The team missed the playoffs for the first time, having been one of only two teams to have qualified on all seven prior occasions.[[20]](#cite_note-our-20)

For the [2004 season](/wiki/2004_Major_League_Soccer_season "2004 Major League Soccer season"), Clarke was named the permanent coach and the team returned to the Cotton Bowl,[[22]](#cite_note-return-22) for a campaign in which they again missed the playoffs. In August, club owner [Lamar Hunt](/wiki/Lamar_Hunt "Lamar Hunt") announced that the club would be re-branded and known as "FC Dallas" to coincide with their new [soccer-specific stadium](/wiki/Soccer-specific_stadium "Soccer-specific stadium") in [Frisco](/wiki/Frisco,_Texas "Frisco, Texas") for the [2005 season](/wiki/2005_Major_League_Soccer_season "2005 Major League Soccer season").[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24)

### FC Dallas era: 2005–present

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Dallas&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: FC Dallas era: 2005–present")]

In March 2005, FC Dallas signed Guatemalan forward [Carlos Ruiz](/wiki/Carlos_Ruiz_(Guatemalan_footballer) "Carlos Ruiz (Guatemalan footballer)"), who had scored 50 goals in 72 games for the Galaxy and earned the MVP award for helping them to the [2002 MLS Cup](/wiki/2002_MLS_Cup "2002 MLS Cup").[[25]](#cite_note-25) On August 6, FC Dallas played their inaugural game at [Pizza Hut Park](/wiki/Pizza_Hut_Park "Pizza Hut Park") and tied the [New York/New Jersey MetroStars](/wiki/MetroStars "MetroStars"), 2–2.[[26]](#cite_note-pizza-26) Ranked second in the West behind the San Jose Earthquakes, Dallas returned to the [playoffs](/wiki/2005_MLS_Cup_Playoffs "2005 MLS Cup Playoffs") for the first time in two seasons, losing in the conference semi-finals to Colorado in a penalty kick shootout, with [Roberto Miña](/wiki/Roberto_Mi%C3%B1a "Roberto Miña")'s attempt saved by [Joe Cannon](/wiki/Joe_Cannon_(soccer) "Joe Cannon (soccer)").[[27]](#cite_note-27) In [2006](/wiki/2006_Major_League_Soccer_season "2006 Major League Soccer season"), the team finished the regular season at the top of the Western Conference,[[28]](#cite_note-28) but lost in the playoffs in the conference semi-finals again, leading to Clarke's dismissal. He was replaced by [Steve Morrow](/wiki/Steve_Morrow "Steve Morrow"). In [2007](/wiki/2007_Major_League_Soccer_season "2007 Major League Soccer season"), a third consecutive [playoff appearance](/wiki/2007_MLS_Cup_Playoffs "2007 MLS Cup Playoffs") ended at the same stage with a 4–2 aggregate defeat to fellow Texas club, the [Houston Dynamo](/wiki/Houston_Dynamo "Houston Dynamo"), who would go on to win their second consecutive MLS Cup.[[29]](#cite_note-29) In [2005](/wiki/2005_Lamar_Hunt_U.S._Open_Cup "2005 Lamar Hunt U.S. Open Cup") and [2007](/wiki/2007_Lamar_Hunt_U.S._Open_Cup "2007 Lamar Hunt U.S. Open Cup"), Dallas reached their first two U.S. Open Cup finals since their 1997 victory, losing both by one-goal margins to the Galaxy and the [New England Revolution](/wiki/New_England_Revolution "New England Revolution") respectively.[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-31) For the following two seasons, Dallas missed the MLS playoffs. During the [2008 season](/wiki/2008_Major_League_Soccer_season "2008 Major League Soccer season"), Morrow was replaced by [Schellas Hyndman](/wiki/Schellas_Hyndman "Schellas Hyndman"). In 2009, the club signed [Bryan Leyva](/wiki/Bryan_Leyva "Bryan Leyva") as the club's first [Homegrown Player](/wiki/Homegrown_Player_Rule_(Major_League_Soccer) "Homegrown Player Rule (Major League Soccer)") from its development academy.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

In [2010](/wiki/2010_MLS_Cup "2010 MLS Cup"), Dallas played in the [MLS Cup](/wiki/MLS_Cup "MLS Cup") for the first time, losing 2–1 after extra time to Colorado at [BMO Field](/wiki/BMO_Field "BMO Field") in Toronto, after an [own goal](/wiki/Own_goal "Own goal") by [George John](/wiki/George_John_(soccer) "George John (soccer)").[[33]](#cite_note-33) They were the last of the surviving original MLS clubs to appear in the MLS Cup final. On-loan Colombian midfielder [David Ferreira](/wiki/David_Ferreira "David Ferreira") was voted the league's MVP, having missed only one minute of the season,[[34]](#cite_note-34) and Hyndman won the [MLS Coach of the Year Award](/wiki/MLS_Coach_of_the_Year_Award "MLS Coach of the Year Award").[[35]](#cite_note-35)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/Oduro_goal.jpg/250px-Oduro_goal.jpg)](/wiki/File:Oduro_goal.jpg)

FC Dallas players celebrating a goal scored by [Dominic Oduro](/wiki/Dominic_Oduro "Dominic Oduro") in a match against Colorado, 2007

By finishing as runners-up in the MLS Cup, Dallas competed in the [2011–12 CONCACAF Champions League](/wiki/2011%E2%80%9312_CONCACAF_Champions_League "2011–12 CONCACAF Champions League"), their first time in the leading continental tournament. Following a victory in the [preliminary round](/wiki/2011%E2%80%9312_CONCACAF_Champions_League_preliminary_round "2011–12 CONCACAF Champions League preliminary round") against [Alianza F.C.](/wiki/Alianza_F.C._(El_Salvador) "Alianza F.C. (El Salvador)") of El Salvador,[[36]](#cite_note-36) they reached the [group stage](/wiki/2011%E2%80%9312_CONCACAF_Champions_League_group_stage "2011–12 CONCACAF Champions League group stage"). In the first group game, [Marvin Chávez](/wiki/Marvin_Ch%C3%A1vez "Marvin Chávez")'s goal defeated Mexican champions [UNAM](/wiki/Pumas_UNAM "Pumas UNAM") at the [Estadio Olímpico Universitario](/wiki/Estadio_Ol%C3%ADmpico_Universitario "Estadio Olímpico Universitario"), making Dallas the first MLS team to win an away match in the Champions League against a Mexican team.[[37]](#cite_note-37) The team followed this achievement with a victory by the same score at [Toronto FC](/wiki/Toronto_FC "Toronto FC"),[[38]](#cite_note-38) but did not win any of their four remaining games and were eliminated from the competition after finishing in third place in their group. In October 2013, Hyndman resigned as head coach after a second consecutive season without making the playoffs.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/Pareja%2C_o.jpg/250px-Pareja%2C_o.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pareja,_o.jpg)

[Óscar Pareja](/wiki/%C3%93scar_Pareja "Óscar Pareja") coached Dallas to the [U.S. Open Cup](/wiki/U.S._Open_Cup "U.S. Open Cup") and [Supporters' Shield](/wiki/Supporters%27_Shield "Supporters' Shield") in 2016

Three months after Hyndman's resignation, his replacement was confirmed to be Colombian and former Dallas player and assistant coach [Óscar Pareja](/wiki/%C3%93scar_Pareja "Óscar Pareja"), who had resigned from the Colorado Rapids after two seasons as head coach there.[[40]](#cite_note-40) Pareja led the club back to the playoffs in [2014](/wiki/2014_Major_League_Soccer_season "2014 Major League Soccer season"). Dallas finished in first place in the Western Conference in [2015](/wiki/2015_Major_League_Soccer_season "2015 Major League Soccer season").[[41]](#cite_note-41) They defeated the [Seattle Sounders FC](/wiki/Seattle_Sounders_FC "Seattle Sounders FC") in the conference semi-finals, only to fall to the [Portland Timbers](/wiki/Portland_Timbers "Portland Timbers") in the Western Conference finals.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

In [2016](/wiki/2016_FC_Dallas_season "2016 FC Dallas season"), Dallas won the U.S. Open Cup with a [4–2 final win](/wiki/2016_U.S._Open_Cup_final "2016 U.S. Open Cup final") over New England on September 13, ending a 19-year trophy drought.[[43]](#cite_note-43) The club ended the [regular season](/wiki/2016_Major_League_Soccer_season "2016 Major League Soccer season") with the [Supporters' Shield](/wiki/Supporters%27_Shield "Supporters' Shield") for best record for the first time,[[44]](#cite_note-44) but fell to Seattle in the Western Conference semi-finals, 4–2 on aggregate.[[45]](#cite_note-45) In the [2016–17 CONCACAF Champions League](/wiki/2016%E2%80%9317_CONCACAF_Champions_League "2016–17 CONCACAF Champions League"), Dallas lost 4–3 on aggregate to eventual champions [Pachuca](/wiki/C.F._Pachuca "C.F. Pachuca") after an added-time goal by [Hirving Lozano](/wiki/Hirving_Lozano "Hirving Lozano").[[46]](#cite_note-46) Dallas lost in the first round of the [2018 MLS Cup playoffs](/wiki/2018_MLS_Cup_playoffs "2018 MLS Cup playoffs") to Portland,[[47]](#cite_note-47) and in the round of 16 of the [year's CONCACAF Champions League](/wiki/2018_CONCACAF_Champions_League "2018 CONCACAF Champions League") to [Tauro](/wiki/Tauro_F.C. "Tauro F.C.") of Panama, on the [away goals rule](/wiki/Away_goals_rule "Away goals rule").[[48]](#cite_note-48) Pareja then left for [Tijuana](/wiki/Club_Tijuana "Club Tijuana").[[49]](#cite_note-49)

Academy director [Luchi Gonzalez](/wiki/Luchi_Gonzalez "Luchi Gonzalez") succeeded Pareja as head coach.[[50]](#cite_note-50) He and the players he had brought through the academy were known as the "Luchi Gang".[[51]](#cite_note-dmag-51) His first two seasons led to playoff qualification and elimination by Seattle, including a 1–0 loss away in the Western Conference semi-finals in [2020](/wiki/2020_MLS_Cup_playoffs "2020 MLS Cup playoffs"),[[52]](#cite_note-52) and he was fired in September 2021 with the club struggling to qualify again.[[51]](#cite_note-dmag-51)

For 2022, Dallas hired a new coach in [Nico Estévez](/wiki/Nico_Est%C3%A9vez "Nico Estévez"), who had been assistant of the [United States men's national team](/wiki/United_States_men%27s_national_soccer_team "United States men's national soccer team").[[53]](#cite_note-53) His first season saw the club finish third in the regular season, before a 2–1 Western Conference semi-final loss to fellow Texans [Austin FC](/wiki/Austin_FC "Austin FC").[[54]](#cite_note-54) His team made the playoffs again in 2023, and he was fired in June 2024 with a 3–8–5 start to the season.[[55]](#cite_note-55)

## Colors and badge

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Dallas&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Colors and badge")]

Originally, the Dallas Burn played in a predominantly red-and-black color scheme, and had a logo which featured a fire-breathing black mustang behind a stylized red "Burn" wordmark.[[7]](#cite_note-fifa-7) The logo and the original colors of red and black were revealed at an event in New York City on October 17, 1995.[[6]](#cite_note-mls96-6)

The team re-branded as FC Dallas in 2005 to coincide with their move to [Pizza Hut Park](/wiki/Pizza_Hut_Park "Pizza Hut Park") in the middle of that season and has since played in a color scheme of red, white, silver, and blue, and a uniform design of horizontally hooped stripes.[[56]](#cite_note-hist-56) The colors are officially listed as Republic Red, Lonestar White, Bovine Blue, and Shawnee Silver.[[57]](#cite_note-57)[[58]](#cite_note-58) Red remained as a primary color in their home uniforms, with blue eventually becoming a primary color of their away uniforms. The club badge was also changed with a bull replacing the mustang. In July 2012, the team wore their first sponsored jerseys, bearing the logo of Texan sports nutrition manufacturers [AdvoCare](/wiki/AdvoCare "AdvoCare").[[59]](#cite_note-59) For the 2014 and 2015 seasons, the hoops were a different shade of red rather than a contrasting white.[[60]](#cite_note-60) The jersey also incorporated the motto "Dallas 'Til I Die" on the inside of the collar and the initials "LH" on the back for Lamar Hunt.[[61]](#cite_note-61)

## Stadium

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Dallas&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Stadium")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/71/Toyota_Stadium.jpeg/250px-Toyota_Stadium.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Toyota_Stadium.jpeg)

[Toyota Stadium](/wiki/Toyota_Stadium_(Texas) "Toyota Stadium (Texas)") in the Dallas suburb of [Frisco](/wiki/Frisco,_Texas "Frisco, Texas")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/CottonBowl.jpg/250px-CottonBowl.jpg)](/wiki/File:CottonBowl.jpg)

FC Dallas played at the [Cotton Bowl](/wiki/Cotton_Bowl_(stadium) "Cotton Bowl (stadium)") from 1996 to 2002; 2004–2005

FC Dallas has had three different home stadiums, each of which has been located in the [Dallas–Fort Worth metroplex](/wiki/Dallas%E2%80%93Fort_Worth_metroplex "Dallas–Fort Worth metroplex").

| Name | Location | Years |
| --- | --- | --- |
| [Cotton Bowl](/wiki/Cotton_Bowl_(stadium) "Cotton Bowl (stadium)") | [Dallas](/wiki/Dallas "Dallas") | 1996–2002 2004–2005 |
| [Dragon Stadium](/wiki/Dragon_Stadium,_Southlake "Dragon Stadium, Southlake") | [Southlake](/wiki/Southlake,_Texas "Southlake, Texas") | 2003 |
| [Toyota Stadium](/wiki/Toyota_Stadium_(Texas) "Toyota Stadium (Texas)") | [Frisco](/wiki/Frisco,_Texas "Frisco, Texas") | 2005–present |

From its foundation, the team played in the 92,100-capacity [Cotton Bowl](/wiki/Cotton_Bowl_(stadium) "Cotton Bowl (stadium)") in [Dallas](/wiki/Dallas "Dallas").[[6]](#cite_note-mls96-6) In an effort to save money due to the club's unfavorable lease with the Cotton Bowl, the club played its 2003 home games at [Dragon Stadium](/wiki/Dragon_Stadium_(Southlake) "Dragon Stadium (Southlake)"), a high school stadium in [Southlake](/wiki/Southlake,_Texas "Southlake, Texas"), a [Fort Worth](/wiki/Fort_Worth "Fort Worth") suburb.[[20]](#cite_note-our-20) After listening to its fans, the team moved back to the Cotton Bowl for the 2004 season.[[22]](#cite_note-return-22)

In August 2005, the club moved to [Toyota Stadium](/wiki/Toyota_Stadium_(Texas) "Toyota Stadium (Texas)"), the third American [soccer-specific stadium](/wiki/Soccer-specific_stadium "Soccer-specific stadium") in the northern suburb of [Frisco](/wiki/Frisco,_Texas "Frisco, Texas"). The stadium's south end was extensively remodeled in 2018, including a new home for the [National Soccer Hall of Fame](/wiki/National_Soccer_Hall_of_Fame "National Soccer Hall of Fame").[[62]](#cite_note-62)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/b/b6/NewToyotaStadium_interior.jpg/250px-NewToyotaStadium_interior.jpg)](/wiki/File:NewToyotaStadium_interior.jpg)

New Toyota Stadium project

In 2025 construction begun on a $182 million redevelopment of the stadium that is scheduled to be completed before the 2028 season. The redevelopment adds roof structures over the east and west stands to provide additional shade, removing the concert stage, developing a new north stand with standing room for the club's "ultras" supporters, and adding what will be the largest LED board in an American soccer-specific stadium.[[63]](#cite_note-63)

## Club culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Dallas&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Club culture")]

### Mascot

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Dallas&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Mascot")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/FC_Dallas_mascot_-_Tex_Hooper.jpg/250px-FC_Dallas_mascot_-_Tex_Hooper.jpg)](/wiki/File:FC_Dallas_mascot_-_Tex_Hooper.jpg)

Tex Hooper, the FC Dallas [mascot](/wiki/Mascot "Mascot")

The mascot of FC Dallas is a bull named Tex Hooper. His fictional biography, by the team, states that he was born on September 6, 1996, in [Frisco, Texas](/wiki/Frisco,_Texas "Frisco, Texas").[[64]](#cite_note-64)

### Supporters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Dallas&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Supporters")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/11/Dallas_fans.JPG/250px-Dallas_fans.JPG)](/wiki/File:Dallas_fans.JPG)

FC Dallas fans enjoy pre-game activities

FC Dallas has two recognized supporters groups: [Dallas Beer Guardians](/wiki/Dallas_Beer_Guardians "Dallas Beer Guardians") and El Matador.[[65]](#cite_note-65)

### Rivalries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Dallas&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Rivalries")]

Main article: [Texas Derby](/wiki/Texas_Derby "Texas Derby")

FC Dallas' main rival is the [Houston Dynamo](/wiki/Houston_Dynamo "Houston Dynamo") in the [Texas Derby](/wiki/Texas_Derby "Texas Derby"). The two teams reside in the same state and compete for El Capitan, a working replica [Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War") cannon that goes to the regular season victor.[[66]](#cite_note-66)

Animosity grew between fans and players of FC Dallas and the [Colorado Rapids](/wiki/Colorado_Rapids "Colorado Rapids"), mainly sparking from Colorado players' comments towards the fans and Colorado's victories over FC Dallas in the [2005](/wiki/2005_MLS_Cup_Playoffs "2005 MLS Cup Playoffs") and [2006 MLS Cup Playoffs](/wiki/2006_MLS_Cup_Playoffs "2006 MLS Cup Playoffs").[[67]](#cite_note-67)

In addition to the Texas Derby, the team also competes in two other [MLS rivalry cups](/wiki/MLS_rivalry_cups "MLS rivalry cups"). The [Brimstone Cup](/wiki/Brimstone_Cup "Brimstone Cup") against the [Chicago Fire](/wiki/Chicago_Fire_Soccer_Club "Chicago Fire Soccer Club"), so named for the allusions to fire in both teams' names when FC Dallas was the Dallas Burn, was inaugurated by the fans in 2001.[[68]](#cite_note-68) The [Lamar Hunt Pioneer Cup](/wiki/Lamar_Hunt_Pioneer_Cup "Lamar Hunt Pioneer Cup") has been contested against [Columbus Crew SC](/wiki/Columbus_Crew_SC "Columbus Crew SC") since 2007. It is named after [Lamar Hunt](/wiki/Lamar_Hunt "Lamar Hunt"), who was an investor in both teams.[[69]](#cite_note-69) Due to league expansion and realignment, FC Dallas only plays Chicago and Columbus once a year now in the regular season, which has led to decreased importance of these two rivalry cups, especially when compared to the Texas Derby.

The burgeoning [Copa Tejas](/wiki/Copa_Tejas "Copa Tejas") rivalry, which pits all three Texas MLS teams, has enhanced the club's rivalry with [Austin FC](/wiki/Austin_FC "Austin FC"). The two have played one playoff match, in [2022](/wiki/2022_MLS_Cup_playoffs "2022 MLS Cup playoffs"), which Austin won.

### Song

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Dallas&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Song")]

During a period where MLS created songs for each club, the team anthem was "H-O-O-P-S Yes!" and was performed by Dallas natives [The Polyphonic Spree](/wiki/The_Polyphonic_Spree "The Polyphonic Spree"), a [choral](/wiki/Choir "Choir") [symphonic rock](/wiki/Symphonic_rock "Symphonic rock") group.[[70]](#cite_note-70)

### Academy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Dallas&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Academy")]

The FC Dallas has produced many talents from the academy including [Weston McKennie](/wiki/Weston_McKennie "Weston McKennie"), [Chris Richards](/wiki/Chris_Richards_(soccer) "Chris Richards (soccer)"), [Nico Carrera](/wiki/Nico_Carrera "Nico Carrera"), [Reggie Cannon](/wiki/Reggie_Cannon "Reggie Cannon"), [Christian Cappis](/wiki/Christian_Cappis "Christian Cappis"), [Jesus Ferreira](/wiki/Jesus_Ferreira "Jesus Ferreira"), [Brandon Servania](/wiki/Brandon_Servania "Brandon Servania"), [Ricardo Pepi](/wiki/Ricardo_Pepi "Ricardo Pepi") and [Bryan Reynolds](/wiki/Bryan_Reynolds_(soccer) "Bryan Reynolds (soccer)"). In 2020, they were ranked the number one academy in MLS by David Kerr on chasingacup.com MLS Academy rankings.[[71]](#cite_note-71)

### Affiliated teams

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Dallas&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Affiliated teams")]

FC Dallas was formally associated with [Oklahoma City Energy FC](/wiki/Oklahoma_City_Energy_FC "Oklahoma City Energy FC") of the [USL Championship](/wiki/USL_Championship "USL Championship"), the second tier of the [American soccer pyramid](/wiki/American_soccer_pyramid "American soccer pyramid").[[72]](#cite_note-72)[[73]](#cite_note-73) They were affiliated with [Arizona United SC](/wiki/Arizona_United_SC "Arizona United SC") of the USL in 2015.[[74]](#cite_note-74)[[75]](#cite_note-75) Abroad, the team was previously affiliated to [Tigres de la UANL](/wiki/Tigres_de_la_UANL "Tigres de la UANL") of Mexico and [Clube Atlético Paranaense](/wiki/Clube_Atl%C3%A9tico_Paranaense "Clube Atlético Paranaense") of Brazil.[[56]](#cite_note-hist-56)

On November 2, 2018, it was announced by [United Soccer League](/wiki/United_Soccer_League "United Soccer League") that Dallas would be granted a side to play in [USL League One](/wiki/USL_League_One "USL League One"), its newly created third division for 2019.[[76]](#cite_note-76) The club then officially announced their name, [North Texas SC](/wiki/North_Texas_SC "North Texas SC"), and crest on December 6, 2018.[[77]](#cite_note-77) The club is owned and operated by FC Dallas and now plays in the [MLS Next Pro](/wiki/MLS_Next_Pro "MLS Next Pro") league.

### Sponsorship

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Dallas&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Sponsorship")]

In 2005, [Pizza Hut](/wiki/Pizza_Hut "Pizza Hut") was the title sponsor of the club's stadium and complex when it opened.[[78]](#cite_note-78) On June 27, 2012, FC Dallas reached a three-year sponsorship deal with [AdvoCare](/wiki/AdvoCare "AdvoCare"), a [Plano](/wiki/Plano,_Texas "Plano, Texas")-based health and wellness company, worth US$7.5M making [AdvoCare](/wiki/AdvoCare "AdvoCare") the official jersey sponsor.[[79]](#cite_note-79) After the [2012 season](/wiki/2012_Major_League_Soccer_season "2012 Major League Soccer season"), [Pizza Hut](/wiki/Pizza_Hut "Pizza Hut") ended their relationship with the club, and the stadium was temporarily renamed as [FC Dallas Stadium](/wiki/FC_Dallas_Stadium "FC Dallas Stadium"). In September 2013 FC Dallas reached a long-term deal with [Toyota](/wiki/Toyota "Toyota") to be official stadium naming rights partners, and the stadium was once again renamed, this time as Toyota Stadium.[[80]](#cite_note-friscoblog-80) In October 2014 FC Dallas and [AdvoCare](/wiki/AdvoCare "AdvoCare") announced an extension of the jersey sponsorship through 2020.[[81]](#cite_note-81) In February 2021, FC Dallas announced [MTX Group](/wiki/MTX "MTX"), a [B2B](/wiki/Business-to-business "Business-to-business") information technology company based in [Frisco](/wiki/Frisco,_Texas "Frisco, Texas"), to be its new shirt sponsor, with Advocare remaining as the team's sleeve sponsor.[[82]](#cite_note-82) In January 2023, FC Dallas announced a sponsorship deal with [Children's Health](/wiki/Children%27s_Health_(health_care_system) "Children's Health (health care system)") and [UT Southwestern](/wiki/University_of_Texas_Southwestern_Medical_Center "University of Texas Southwestern Medical Center") to be its new jersey sponsors.[[83]](#cite_note-83)

### Television

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Dallas&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Television")]

Since the 2023 season, all live FC Dallas matches stream on [MLS Season Pass](/wiki/MLS_Season_Pass "MLS Season Pass") as part of the league's exclusive broadcast rights agreement with [Apple Inc.](/wiki/Apple_Inc. "Apple Inc."), with selected matches able to be televised by [Fox Sports](/wiki/Fox_Sports_(United_States) "Fox Sports (United States)") platforms as part of a sub-licensing agreement.[[85]](#cite_note-Inquirer-Univision-85)[[86]](#cite_note-86)[[87]](#cite_note-:2-87)

Prior to the all-streaming deal, the club's non-nationally televised games were primarily broadcast in Dallas on local channel [KTXA](/wiki/KTXA "KTXA"). This arrangement began with the [2015 season](/wiki/2015_FC_Dallas_season "2015 FC Dallas season").[[88]](#cite_note-:0-88) The club struggled for years to find consistent broadcast partners in the crowded Dallas–Fort Worth sports market. In August 2018, FC Dallas launched the FCDTV Network, comprising local stations [KJBO-LP](/wiki/KJBO-LD "KJBO-LD") (Amarillo), [KMYL-LD](/wiki/KMYL-LD "KMYL-LD") (Lubbock), [KTPN-LD](/wiki/KTPN-LD "KTPN-LD") (Tyler-Longview) and [KJBO-LP](/wiki/KJBO-LD "KJBO-LD") (Wichita Falls/Lawton).[[89]](#cite_note-89) Due to scheduling conflicts with KTXA during the return of [2020 Major League Soccer season](/wiki/2020_Major_League_Soccer_season "2020 Major League Soccer season") from the COVID-19 pandemic, select matches of FC Dallas were moved to [Fox Sports Southwest](/wiki/Fox_Sports_Southwest "Fox Sports Southwest").[[90]](#cite_note-90)

On February 25, 2013, FC Dallas signed a deal with [Time Warner Cable](/wiki/Time_Warner_Cable "Time Warner Cable") to air most of its games on the [Time Warner Cable Sports Channel](/wiki/Time_Warner_Cable_Sports_Channel_(Texas) "Time Warner Cable Sports Channel (Texas)") in Dallas, replacing [Fox Sports Southwest](/wiki/Fox_Sports_Southwest "Fox Sports Southwest") as the primary broadcaster of games.[[91]](#cite_note-broadcast-91) This arrangement lasted for two seasons. It was not popular with fans as the channel was not available on many cable and satellite packages besides those offered by Time Warner. The channel still broadcasts some games that are not broadcast by KTXA.[[92]](#cite_note-92) Also, in some areas outside of the Dallas–Fort Worth market, the channel continues to broadcast the club's games.

Until the [2012 season](/wiki/2012_Major_League_Soccer_season "2012 Major League Soccer season"), FC Dallas matches appeared on various local television stations such as [KTXA](/wiki/KTXA "KTXA") and [WFAA](/wiki/WFAA "WFAA") (digital channel 8.3), and regional sports network [Fox Sports Southwest](/wiki/Fox_Sports_Southwest "Fox Sports Southwest") (often on alternate Fox Sports Southwest Plus channels when conflicting with [Texas Rangers](/wiki/Texas_Rangers_(baseball) "Texas Rangers (baseball)"), [Dallas Mavericks](/wiki/Dallas_Mavericks "Dallas Mavericks"), and [Dallas Stars](/wiki/Dallas_Stars "Dallas Stars") games).

In 2012, [Dallas Mavericks](/wiki/Dallas_Mavericks "Dallas Mavericks") play-by-play announcer [Mark Followill](/wiki/Mark_Followill "Mark Followill") also became the primary play-by-play announcer for FC Dallas, replacing the late [Bobby Rhine](/wiki/Bobby_Rhine "Bobby Rhine"). Former [Houston Dynamo](/wiki/Houston_Dynamo "Houston Dynamo") announcer Jonathan Yardley shared play-by-play responsibilities with Followill in 2012. In 2013, Bob Sturm (weekday early afternoon co-host on sports radio [KTCK](/wiki/KTCK_(AM) "KTCK (AM)")) replaced Yardley, who has continued to fill in for Followill and Sturm in 2013, 2016, and 2018. The color commentator spot was filled until 2016 by a rotation of former MLS players including: [Brian Dunseth](/wiki/Brian_Dunseth "Brian Dunseth"), [Ian Joy](/wiki/Ian_Joy "Ian Joy"), [Kevin Hartman](/wiki/Kevin_Hartman "Kevin Hartman"), [Steve Jolley](/wiki/Steve_Jolley "Steve Jolley"), and [Dante Washington](/wiki/Dante_Washington "Dante Washington"). FC Dallas employee Daniel Robertson or Sturm (beginning in 2016) filled in when one of the others are not available.[[93]](#cite_note-93) Longtime national soccer writer Steve Davis has been the analyst on all matches since 2018. Beginning with the new KTXA deal in 2015, longtime local sports broadcaster Gina Miller hosted a team produced 30-minute pregame show on select broadcasts.

In 2021, FC Dallas announced that [Estrella TV](/wiki/Estrella_TV "Estrella TV") would become the first team's regional Spanish television partner for the 2021 and 2022 seasons, with matches appearing on Estrella's Dallas affiliate [KMPX](/wiki/KMPX "KMPX").[[94]](#cite_note-94)

In 2025, invoking an option in the Apple contract that allows for regional rebroadcasts of matches on at least a 48-hour delay, FC Dallas announced that it would air Tuesday-night encores of matches on [KDFI](/wiki/KDFI "KDFI") under the *FC Dallas Rewind* branding.[[87]](#cite_note-:2-87)

### Radio

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Dallas&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Radio")]

Beginning with the 2018 season,[[95]](#cite_note-95) English radio coverage of the club's MLS matches has been on the club's website.[[96]](#cite_note-:1-96) Beginning with the 2019 season for locally televised games, the radio coverage has been a simulcast of the audio from the television broadcast.[[97]](#cite_note-97) Starting in 2023, FC Dallas began originating English language commentary through their website before broadcasting games on Talk Radio 1190AM through the 2024 season. Beginning in 2025 the club began streaming their English commentary on YouTube, creating a second screen experience called "The Burncast" while also broadcasting on their app.

Carlos Alvarado and Rafa Calderon provide Spanish language commentary on radio stations such as [KFLC](/wiki/KFLC "KFLC") and [KFZO](/wiki/KFZO "KFZO").[[88]](#cite_note-:0-88)[[98]](#cite_note-98) Alvarado has been the play-by-play announcer since the inaugural 1996 season, and Calderon has been the color analyst since the 2001 season.[[96]](#cite_note-:1-96) The club discontinued Spanish language commentary ahead of the 2025 season.
